topic.what: Comprehensive review proposing that zonulin, a physiological modulator of tight junction opening, drives increased intestinal permeability, which in turn serves as a gateway to systemic inflammatory and autoimmune diseases when chronically dysregulated.
topic.how: Synthesizes the author's own research program alongside the broader literature on tight junction biology; presents a mechanistic model in which dietary antigens (gluten via gliadin) and microbial signals (LPS) activate zonulin release, opening paracellular spaces, allowing antigen translocation, and triggering adaptive immune responses. Central to the functional medicine 'leaky gut' framework.
topic.why_matters: Provides the most cited mechanistic account of intestinal permeability as a disease driver, moving the concept from speculation to a testable molecular pathway. Relevant to gastritis because gastric mucosal barrier disruption shares conceptual architecture with intestinal tight-junction dysregulation, and because autoimmune gastritis involves immune activation at epithelial surfaces.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which the zonulin-tight junction-permeability cascade is molecularly defined
characteristics.value: Strong — receptor, signaling cascade, and downstream effects specified
characteristics.vs_baseline: Earlier 'leaky gut' literature was largely phenomenological without molecular mechanism
characteristics.evidence: Author's own experimental work plus corroborating studies on tight junction proteins (occludin, claudin, ZO-1)
characteristics.context: Zonulin's precise molecular identity has been debated; some findings require independent replication

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number of distinct autoimmune/inflammatory conditions linked to zonulin-mediated permeability
characteristics.value: High — celiac disease, T1 diabetes, IBD, multiple sclerosis, autism spectrum reviewed
characteristics.vs_baseline: Prior work largely limited to celiac disease as the exemplar condition
characteristics.evidence: Cross-disease comparative review with primary citations
characteristics.context: Causal direction (permeability → disease vs. disease → permeability) not always established

characteristics.what_it_measures: Readiness of zonulin pathway as therapeutic target
characteristics.value: Moderate — larazotide acetate (zonulin antagonist) in clinical trials for celiac disease
characteristics.vs_baseline: No approved therapeutics targeting tight junctions as of review
characteristics.evidence: Phase II trial data cited for larazotide
characteristics.context: Translational work focused on celiac; gastritis-specific applications not yet explored

good_for: Building the molecular rationale for intestinal permeability as a disease mechanism
Connecting microbiome dysbiosis to immune activation via barrier disruption
Informing functional medicine frameworks for GI inflammatory conditions including gastritis
Identifying zonulin as a measurable serum biomarker for gut barrier integrity
not_for: Clinical diagnosis of 'leaky gut' — no validated diagnostic threshold exists
Direct application to gastric (versus intestinal) barrier biology without extrapolation
Conditions where permeability is a secondary epiphenomenon of disease
requires: Critical reading: zonulin's molecular identity (complement C3, haptoglobin-2 precursor) has been contested
Awareness that Fasano is both the primary researcher and reviewer — independent replication important

novelty: Established zonulin as the first identified physiological regulator of intestinal tight junctions, giving the 'leaky gut' concept a named molecular mediator
Created a unified framework linking diverse autoimmune diseases through a shared gut barrier mechanism
Provided the conceptual and experimental basis for tight-junction modulation as a therapeutic strategy
open_problems: The precise molecular identity of zonulin remains disputed in the literature
Directionality question: whether barrier disruption causes autoimmunity or vice versa is unresolved for most conditions
Validated diagnostic cutoffs for serum zonulin as a clinical biomarker do not exist
Application of the framework to gastric mucosal barrier (H. pylori, NSAID injury, autoimmune gastritis) is underexplored
Population-level variation in tight junction regulation and zonulin responsiveness is unstudied
